forecasts.reasoning: The bar for YES is extremely high: with the latest known baseline at 9.833, Honduras would need roughly 99+ Strategic developments in a 30-day window, versus a trailing norm of about 9 per 30 days. Evidence up to 2025-10-26 shows a tense 2025 electoral cycle—chaotic March primaries, military/logistics disputes, observer concern, multiple candidate killings, and a high-stakes Nov. 30 general election—but most likely election-related surges would appear in other ACLED categories (protests, riots, violence) or still fall well short of an 11x spike in Strategic developments. I therefore keep the near-term window at the floor-like low end, raise slightly for the final pre-election month, and raise more for the post-election / pre-inauguration window because contested results, recounts, deployments, and institutional disputes could create more strategic-development coding, though still probably not enough to clear ~99 events.

Monaco looks like an extreme low-riot environment. The current reference average for ACLED 'Riots' is 0.000, so this is effectively asking whether there will be at least one ACLED-coded riot in each 30-day window. I’m cutting the 0.225 generic base rate sharply downward because: (1) Monaco police/security reporting describes very low violent crime and strong control capacity, with only four violent crimes in 2024 and a very large police/surveillance presence for a tiny population (lookup_0); (2) the recent protest activity I found in Monaco consists of small, organized, peaceful union demonstrations, including recurring rallies in 2023 and 2024, not riot-like events (search_1_summary); and (3) I found no evidence of ongoing October 2025 unrest in Monaco. For 2025-11-02, most of the 30-day window is already effectively known by the cutoff and there is no sign of riots, so I use the floor 0.05. I raise the later dates only slightly, to reflect greater uncertainty about unforeseen shocks while keeping them well below the overall base rate.

Base rate for 10x ACLED spikes is extremely low, and this case looks especially hard to trigger: the reference value is about 1.0, implying the YES threshold is effectively 11+ strategic developments in 30 days. The prompt’s own latest known reference (1.000 as of 2025-10-16) suggests Lesotho had essentially no strategic developments in the prior year, so a sudden 11-event burst would require a sharp regime crisis, coup scare, or similar cascade. Lesotho does have chronic political instability, stalled reforms, and some reporting/snippets indicating opposition no-confidence and security-sector tension around Matekane, plus economic stress in 2025. But as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff I do not see strong, well-confirmed evidence of an active nationwide crisis already producing strategic-development volume near the needed threshold. So I stay near the floor for the near date, with only a small increase for later dates to reflect greater uncertainty and more time for an unforeseen political/security flare-up.

Starting from the 0.225 base rate, I adjust upward because Israel has an unusually dense, persistent protest infrastructure and late Sep/Oct 2025 still featured repeated multi-city weekly demonstrations, including large Tel Aviv rallies and organized events in Jerusalem/Haifa. That means the 2025-11-02 window is partly 'preloaded' with substantial protest activity already visible by the 2025-10-26 cutoff. But the bar is high: the comparison value is already about 128.25 protest events per 30 days, and the October ceasefire/hostage-release developments appear to have cooled or transformed some mobilization into celebration rather than protest. So I think exceeding that benchmark is plausible but still less likely than not. For the later dates, I move probabilities modestly toward 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty: there is room for renewed anti-government / hostage / judicial protests to rebound, but also a real chance the post-deal lull persists.

Starting from the 0.225 base rate, I adjust upward because conflict indicators in eastern DRC were clearly elevated by the 2025-10-26 cutoff: repeated FARDC drone/air strikes on M23 positions in October (including reported strikes on Oct. 8, 15, 21, and 23), continuing ceasefire violations, M23 recruitment, and ADF expansion (search_0_summary, search_1_summary, search_2_summary). That raises the odds that 30-day 'Explosions/Remote violence' totals will beat the reference level. But the bar to clear is also fairly high: the known rolling reference was about 11.25 events per 30 days as of 2025-10-16, and that annual baseline already incorporates an exceptionally violent 2025, including the deadliest quarter since 2002. So despite current escalation, exceeding the benchmark is still below even odds. I give the nearest date the highest evidence-based confidence from already-known late-October strikes, then move modestly closer to 0.5 at longer horizons to reflect growing uncertainty while preserving the same directional view.

The fixed comparison level is about 3.167 protests per 30 days as of 2025-10-16, and the final 2025-10-26 reference is unlikely to move enough to change the practical threshold: this likely means 4+ ACLED protest events are needed in the target 30-day window. Martinique had a major protest wave in Sep-Oct 2024 and additional protest activity in Jan-March 2025, showing the issue is real and can flare up. But searches up to the 2025-10-26 cutoff show little clear evidence of another high-frequency protest wave in late 2025, despite persistent cost-of-living grievances and still-rising prices. So exceeding a high bar of 4 protests in 30 days looks less likely than not. I put the nearest date lowest because most of that 30-day window was already effectively determined by the cutoff and there is no strong sign of a late-October surge. I raise the probability modestly for later dates because unresolved economic grievances, possible renewed mobilization, and general forecast uncertainty increase with horizon, but not enough to make YES more likely than NO.

The threshold is extremely high. Using the latest known reference value of 3.167, Hungary would need roughly 32+ ACLED 'Strategic developments' in a 30-day window. That is more strategic developments in one month than Hungary had in the prior 360 days combined implied by the same baseline (~26 total). Hungary is politically tense ahead of the April 2026 election, with large rival rallies, a possible NGO/media crackdown bill, Sovereignty Protection Office activity, EU Article 7 pressure, and possible court rulings. But most visible mobilization is likely to be coded as protests, not strategic developments. The plausible strategic-development flow seems like a handful of events, not 30+. So I stay near the floor for all dates, with a small increase over time because a longer horizon gives somewhat more chance of an acute election-related constitutional or governance crisis producing an unusual cluster of strategic developments.

Akrotiri and Dhekelia appear to have an extremely low underlying ACLED-riot rate: the known 360-day reference average was 0.000, and the protest activity I found around RAF Akrotiri in 2024-2025 was consistently described as peaceful demonstrations/direct actions rather than riots. That said, anti-base and Gaza/Yemen-related activism is persistent, so a small tail risk of escalation remains. I put the first date lowest because by the 2025-10-26 forecast date most of that 30-day window had already elapsed with no sign of riot activity; the later dates are slightly higher mainly because there is more time for renewed protests or clashes, especially around ongoing regional tensions and January-related military flashpoints, but still well below the generic base rate because this is a tiny territory with no recent riot history.

The bar is very high: with the latest known reference at 1.000, this likely requires roughly 11+ ACLED 'Battle' events in a 30-day window. Solomon Islands has had recent risks of unrest, riots, and protests tied to politics/China relations, but the evidence found points much more to disorder, looting, and policing concerns than to sustained armed-group-versus-armed-group fighting that ACLED would code as Battles. Historical militia conflict ('the Tensions') is old; 2024 elections created tension but did not produce such a battle spike; 2025 reporting emphasizes surveillance, reconciliation, and unrest prevention rather than active organized combat. So YES is very unlikely on all dates. I put the earliest date at the floor because there was little time after the forecast date for 11+ battles to emerge, and I nudge probabilities up slightly for later dates to reflect greater horizon uncertainty while keeping them low overall.

The effective threshold is almost certainly at least 1 fatality in the 30-day window, since the known reference average on 2025-10-16 was 0.500 and it is very unlikely ACLED revisions by 2025-10-26 would push the 360-day average above 1.0. So this is basically a forecast of whether Azerbaijan records any ACLED fatalities in each target 30-day period.

I start from the empirical base rate (0.225). I adjust somewhat upward because Azerbaijan is not a quiet case: there are continuing Armenia-Azerbaijan ceasefire violations and persistent landmine/legacy-explosive risk in retaken territories. But I adjust downward from a simple raw-rate extrapolation because the best reporting up to the cutoff indicates no border fatalities since February 2024, and by mid/late 2025 the peace process had improved materially (agreement initialed in August 2025, leaders meeting in September, reciprocal border visits, dialogue intensifying). That suggests current intentional-violence risk is lower than a naive reading of the backward-looking 0.500 average might imply.

For 2025-11-02, only the last week of the 30-day window is still unknown as of the cutoff; absent evidence of early-October fatalities, this makes YES less likely. For 2025-11-25, the whole 30-day window is still ahead, so probability rises. For 2026-01-24, I move slightly closer to 0.5 because longer-horizon uncertainty is higher, though the central estimate remains below even odds given the relatively low current fatality tempo.

The reference value was 1.083 as of 2025-10-16, which implies ACLED had only about 1 Canada 'Explosions/Remote violence' event in the prior 360 days; even with revisions by 2025-10-26, the trigger is likely still roughly 11+ events in a 30-day window (and possibly 12+ if the denominator rises). Recent Canada reporting before 2025-10-26 shows isolated incidents or disrupted plots (e.g. a live grenade incident, bomb threats, militia/explosives arrests), not an ongoing campaign of repeated remote-violence events. Canada’s threat environment is nonzero, but sustained 30-day counts above 10x baseline would require a dramatic and unusual escalation from an extremely low base. So I keep all three probabilities near the imposed floor, with only a slight increase for the longer horizons because there is more time for a rare cluster to emerge.

Baseline reference is already high: as of 2025-10-16 the 360-day 30-day-average for Mozambique 'Strategic developments' was about 20.167, so resolution likely requires roughly 21+ events in the target 30-day window. That is a fairly demanding bar because the comparison period already includes the post-election crisis, protests/crackdown, ongoing Cabo Delgado insurgency, and repeated political/security announcements. I therefore keep all forecasts below 50%. I adjust upward from the generic 0.225 base rate because Mozambique in late 2025 still had multiple active drivers of ACLED strategic-development entries: continued insurgent adaptation and infrastructure disruption, national dialogue after the election crisis, and the major 2025-10-25 lifting of force majeure on the Mozambique LNG project. For 2025-11-02, the window overlaps substantially with late Sep/Oct 2025, when these dynamics were clearly active, so odds are meaningfully above the generic prior but still below even. For 2025-11-25 and 2026-01-24, I expect uncertainty to increase; those probabilities move modestly toward 0.5 as the future path could involve either cooling or fresh bursts of strategic activity.

This looks very unlikely. The prompt’s latest reference value is 10.5, implying a 30-day average of about 9.5 'Violence against civilians' events over the prior 360 days in Venezuela, so a YES would require roughly 105+ such events in a 30-day window. That is an enormous jump relative to the recent background rate. The strongest case for a spike is the sustained repression after the disputed 2024 election: UN/HRW reporting indicates killings, disappearances, arbitrary detentions, and continued politically motivated abuses through 2025, including involvement of security forces and pro-government armed civilians. But even that severe crackdown does not obviously suggest ACLED event counts anywhere near 105 in 30 days. For 2025-11-02, the horizon is very short and there was no clear evidence by 2025-10-26 of an ongoing nationwide surge on that scale, so I use the floor. For 2025-11-25, risk is slightly higher because repression could intensify as municipal elections approach. For 2026-01-24, I raise it a bit more because that window could capture municipal-election and post-election unrest plus greater forecast uncertainty, but the threshold remains so extreme that NO is still overwhelmingly more likely.

The fixed comparison threshold is high: the latest known 30-day-average-over-360-days for Bangladesh was about 31.167 fatalities, and that trailing year includes the very deadly 2024 protest/repression period, which likely inflates the benchmark. That makes a YES harder than the raw Bangladesh violence environment might suggest. Still, Bangladesh remained unusually violent through 2025: reporting before the cutoff indicates roughly 107 political-violence deaths, 130 mob-violence/lynching deaths, and 23 border-violence deaths in the first nine months of 2025 (about 29/month in aggregate, though not all will map perfectly into ACLED), plus 160 political-violence deaths over Sep 2024-Sep 2025 and persistent communal/revenge violence. So the relevant monthly fatality level appears in the same ballpark as the threshold, but a bit below it. For 2025-11-02, only a short unseen tail remains after the forecast date, and there was no strong evidence of a major late-October spike large enough to make YES more likely than NO. For 2025-11-25 and especially 2026-01-24, the horizon is longer and election-related tensions under the interim government create some upside risk of a renewed spike, so I move modestly toward 0.5 with distance.

Mongolia looks like a very low-fatality ACLED environment as of 2025-10-26. The prompt notes the reference value was 1.000 on 2025-10-16, implying essentially no fatalities in the preceding 360 days, so the trigger is roughly 11+ fatalities in a 30-day window. Recent 2025 unrest is real but appears mostly protest-driven and non-lethal: reporting on the May-June anti-PM protests describes weeks of demonstrations and a government collapse, but no fatalities; late-October reporting describes a constitutional/political crisis and growing protests/strikes, again without reported deaths. Mongolia also lacks an active insurgency/terrorism problem in the evidence gathered. Historical precedent for deadly unrest exists but is rare: 2008 riots killed about 5, still below this question’s threshold, while the 2022 corruption protests appear to have caused injuries but not deaths. For 2025-11-02, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the cutoff, and there was no evidence of fatalities, so a YES would likely require a sudden 11+ deaths in the final week—very unlikely. Risk rises modestly for 2025-11-25 and again for 2026-01-24 because the political crisis could still escalate into riots or a harsh crackdown, but even then exceeding 10 fatalities in a month remains unlikely. I therefore keep all forecasts at the very low end, increasing slightly with horizon.

Baseline is unusually high: the known reference around 2025-10-16 was ~435 protest events per 30 days, reflecting Turkey’s exceptionally intense 2025 protest cycle. Exceeding that requires another very strong nationwide wave, not just a few large rallies. Evidence before 2025-10-26 shows protests remained active through 2025 (Imamoglu-related mobilization, repeated CHP rallies, student activism, labor unrest, and a major September opposition rally), so the chance is above a bare ‘quiet-country’ prior. But October headlines suggest activity was still elevated yet not clearly at March-peak intensity, making an above-baseline 30-day total still more likely NO than YES. I therefore put the nearest date below 30%, raise the 2025-11-25 probability somewhat because of the scheduled early-November CHP court hearing and continuing labor/political flashpoints, and keep the farthest date only slightly higher—reflecting greater uncertainty (closer to 0.5) but also no specific evidence that winter 2026 protest counts will decisively exceed this already-high benchmark.

The reference rate is very low: the latest known 360-day baseline average was 0.083 per 30 days, which effectively means roughly 1 ACLED Explosions/Remote violence event in the prior year. So for all three dates, the question is basically asking whether there is at least one such event in the relevant 30-day window. Chile does have persistent security problems in the south (Mapuche conflict), and reporting before 2025-10-26 suggests violence remains elevated, conflict is spreading into Los Ríos/Los Lagos, and radical groups remain active. But most of that violence appears to be arson, shootings, and intimidation rather than the specific ACLED Explosions/Remote violence category, which seems rare in Chile. That pushes me below the generic 0.225 prior. For 2025-11-02, much of the 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, and absent evidence of a known qualifying incident I keep the probability low. For 2025-11-25, a full future 30-day window plus election-period tensions raise the chance somewhat. For 2026-01-24, I move a bit closer to 0.5 because horizon uncertainty is larger and conflict dynamics could shift, but the base rate still argues for a low probability overall.

Threshold is roughly 11+ strategic-development events in a 30-day window, and possibly 12 if ACLED backfills before fixing the 2025-10-26 reference value. The strongest clue is that the known reference value of 1.083 implies only about one strategic-development event in the prior 360 days, so ACLED has recently coded this event type very sparsely in New Caledonia. There is real tail risk from the unresolved constitutional dispute: the July 2025 Bougival accord, FLNKS’s August rejection, the September election postponement, the Constitutional Council ruling, and the FLNKS Oct. 30 ballot-demand deadline could all generate additional political/administrative events. But even renewed instability would more naturally produce protests/riots/violence rather than 11+ ACLED strategic developments in a single month, and I found no evidence by 2025-10-26 of a late-October cascade already underway. So I keep the forecast near the bottom of the allowed range, with only a slight increase for the later dates because uncertainty rises with horizon.

Baseline starts at the empirical prior of 0.225, but Côte d’Ivoire had a major election-driven exception in late Oct 2025. The trailing 360-day reference was 4.750 as of Oct 16, so the practical hurdle is roughly 5 strategic-development events in a 30-day window (possibly 6 if later ACLED revisions push the baseline up a bit). Evidence before the cutoff shows an unusually dense cluster of strategic-type developments around the 25 Oct presidential election: candidate exclusions, protest bans, deployment of 44,000 security personnel, attempted banned protests, hundreds of arrests, opposition calls for daily protests, and then the election/result-processing period itself. That makes the 2025-11-02 window, which captures most of that election surge, better than even to clear the threshold. For 2025-11-25, the window is mostly post-election aftermath; certification/disputes/arrests/dialogue could still create 5+ events, but much of the pre-election spike is no longer in-window, so I put it below even odds. By 2026-01-24 there is no specific identified flashpoint in the target month, but uncertainty is higher and the threshold is near the country’s recent monthly level, so I move the probability back toward 0.5 while keeping it slightly below even due to likely post-election cooling.

This looks overwhelmingly likely to resolve NO on all three dates. The current fixed reference value is about 1.000, so the practical threshold is roughly 11+ ACLED-coded Battles in the prior 30 days. That is an extremely high bar for Antarctica. Question-specific evidence is strongly negative: Antarctica is governed by the Antarctic Treaty, major analysts describe armed-conflict fears there as largely speculative/"phantom threats," and the only notable 2025 incident I found was an alleged assault at Sanae IV, which is not an ACLED Battle because it is not fighting between organized armed groups (search_2_summary, search_3_summary). Historical context from multiple sources also emphasizes that actual battles in Antarctica are essentially unheard of. Given the empirical 10x-spike prior of 0.002 and the even more restrictive local conditions here, my unconstrained estimate would be below 5%; because the instructions require probabilities >=0.05, I use the minimum for the nearest date and only nudge upward with horizon to reflect greater uncertainty farther out.

Primary anchor is the FRED BAA10Y series. As of 2025-10-24 the spread was 1.65, down from ~1.75 in early September and below much of the mid-2025 range. The tool’s combined statistical estimate for 2025-11-02 is 0.52 for exceeding 1.65, with a positive linear-model forecast but recent downward momentum. I nudge that slightly upward because the late-October level looks a bit low relative to the recent several-month average, so modest mean reversion/widening is plausible. The one web search gave mixed qualitative evidence: 2025 outlook pieces emphasized volatility and possible spread widening if growth weakens, but also noted spreads were fairly tight and could stay tight absent a shock. So I see only a slight edge toward an increase, not a strong one. Per instructions, uncertainty rises with horizon, so later probabilities are pulled closer to 0.5.

Primary anchor: FRED trend analysis on BAMLC0A2CAAEY gave a combined P(increase above the 2025-10-26 comparison value, proxied by 4.38) of 0.62 for 2025-11-02. Recent data show the yield fell into late October but was still oscillating around 4.38-4.40, so a small bounce above the threshold over the next week is slightly more likely than not. One web search added market context: Reuters/market commentary before the cutoff said long Treasury yields were expected to stay elevated (10Y around 4.10 in 3-6 months), term premium was high, and IG spreads were very tight. That supports a mild upward bias in AA corporate yields, though tight spreads and possible Fed cuts limit conviction. I therefore keep the near-term probability close to the FRED anchor, then move closer to 0.5 at longer horizons to reflect greater uncertainty: 0.61 for 2025-11-02, 0.58 for 2025-11-25, and 0.55 for 2026-01-24.

I trim that anchor slightly because the exact 2025-10-26 reference value is not directly observed and could differ marginally from 4.88.

One Reuters-based search adds that, as of mid/late October 2025, long Treasury yields were expected to remain elevated or edge higher over coming months because sticky inflation and debt pressures were limiting how much longer-dated yields could fall, even as the Fed cut at the short end. Since BBB corporate effective yield is Treasury yield plus credit spread, that creates a mild upward bias. Offsetting that, BBB spreads were described as tight and subject to a bumpy ride, so spread tightening could partially cancel higher Treasury yields.

Net: short-term probability is modestly above 60%; then I move probabilities closer to 0.5 with horizon as uncertainty rises, while keeping a slight YES lean because the macro/rates backdrop points to stable-to-slightly-higher yields rather than a clear decline.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool on CC4WSA through the 2025-10-26 cutoff: last observed level was about 1.948M on 2025-10-25, with a combined model estimate of 0.47 that the next point exceeds that threshold. I adjust that upward for the near term because the recent path was clearly rising (4 of last 5 points up; strong positive linear slope), and the one allowed web search surfaced contemporaneous reporting on 2025-10-23 that continuing claims were increasing, which supports short-run upward momentum. Against that, seasonality around late autumn/early winter is not strongly favorable and the same-period-prior-year comparison is weak/negative, so I do not go very high. For longer horizons, uncertainty rises and I pull probabilities back toward 0.5 as instructed. Final estimates: modestly more likely than not by 2025-11-02, only slightly more likely by 2025-11-25, and nearly a coin flip by 2026-01-24.

Primary anchor: the FRED tool’s combined estimate for the near-term comparison against the late-October baseline (~1055.6/1056) is 0.55 for 2025-11-02. Primary anchor is the FRED trend output for CREACBW027SBOG as of 2025-10-26: last observed value was about 3039 on 2025-10-22, and the tool’s combined estimate for being above that level by 2025-11-02 was 0.36. I adjust that slightly upward to 0.39 because the series has still drifted up over the past year (roughly +35B y/y) and recent weekly moves, while noisy, have been mildly positive overall. Qualitative evidence is mixed: Trepp reports bank-balance-sheet CRE origination volumes increased in Q2 2025, but the Fed/SLOOS evidence shows tighter or unchanged standards and weaker or unchanged demand for CRE loans (search_0_summary). That argues against a strong bullish move from an already elevated late-October level. For 2025-11-25 and 2026-01-24, cumulative upward drift makes an increase versus the late-October baseline somewhat more likely, but because the current reading is already high and medium-term uncertainty rises, I keep the longer-horizon probabilities only modestly closer to 0.5 rather than high-conviction YES.

Primary anchor is the FRED DFII10 tool output: with threshold 1.73, it gives a combined P(increase by 2025-11-02) of 0.63, based on a near-flat recent trend, modest positive linear-model signal, and prior-year seasonal comparison. I treat that as the short-horizon base case. Qualitatively, pre-cutoff reporting points in two directions: (1) labor-market softness and Powell’s employment-focused easing bias were pulling nominal Treasury yields lower in late October, which argues against a large rise in real yields; but (2) Reuters polling and strategist commentary still expected long-end yields to stay relatively elevated because of sticky inflation, fiscal/debt pressures, and high term premium, while SF Fed commentary suggested inflation expectations were not surging. That mix slightly favors real yields staying above the late-October level, but not by much. So I set the 1-week probability near the tool anchor at 0.62, then pull probabilities closer to 0.5 at longer horizons to reflect greater uncertainty and mean reversion: 0.58 by 2025-11-25 and 0.55 by 2026-01-24.

Primary anchor: the FRED tool’s short-horizon model, using the latest known pre-cutoff observation as the comparison threshold (3.76 on 2025-10-24), gives about a 0.68 chance of being above that level by 2025-11-02. I adjust that down materially because the front end was already reflecting strong expectations of an October Fed cut and meaningful odds of another December cut by the 2025-10-26 cutoff; those expectations lean against higher 6-month yields. Still, because much of the easing path was likely already priced into the 3.76 level, I do not move far below 50%. So I put the nearest date slightly above even odds, the late-November date below 50% as expected easing weighs more on the 6-month point, and the January date a bit closer to 0.5 again because the longer horizon adds more two-sided uncertainty about the 2026 path.

Primary anchor is the FRED trend model, which gives about a 0.31 chance that DJIA is above the relevant baseline by 2025-11-02. The effective comparison level appears to be the last pre-10/26 close, 47,207.12 on 2025-10-24, which is a high bar after a strong late-October run-up. That makes a near-term pullback or sideways move fairly plausible. I nudge slightly above the model because recent momentum was still positive and the broader macro backdrop was mildly supportive: the Fed had started a rate-cut cycle, inflation/yield expectations were easing, and the soft-landing narrative remained intact. Offsetting that are stretched valuations, tariff/policy uncertainty, and correction risk after a sharp rally. For the later dates, I move probabilities modestly toward 0.5: longer horizons give more time for positive equity drift and easier policy to help, but per instruction uncertainty should increase with horizon, so I keep the farther-date forecasts closer to 0.5 rather than making them more extreme.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool’s trend analysis for DPSACBW027SBOG, which gave a combined estimate of 0.49 for exceeding the comparison level by 2025-11-02. I adjust slightly upward because the series has shown a clear medium-term rise through 2025 (roughly +600 to +700 bn over the past year, with a positive linear slope), so the underlying drift is favorable to a higher level than late October. So I put the near-term probability just above even. Per instruction, uncertainty increases with horizon, so the later probabilities move back toward 0.5 rather than farther away: 2025-11-25 still has a small positive edge from trend, and 2026-01-24 is only slightly above 50-50 because longer-horizon uncertainty and possible seasonal/revision effects offset the upward drift.

Primary anchor: the FRED DTB4WK trend tool, using data through 2025-10-24, showed the latest known value at 3.94 and a short-run statistical estimate of 0.66 for being above that threshold by 2025-11-02. However, I adjust materially downward because question-specific macro evidence before the cutoff points the other way: Reuters reporting on Oct. 20-24, 2025 said economists/traders broadly expected a 25 bp Fed cut the following week and another in December. The 4-week bill rate is very sensitive to the expected average policy rate over the next month, so anticipated easing should lean against an increase versus the late-October baseline. Recent momentum in the series was also downward. For 2025-11-25, one expected cut would already be in place and a December cut would be partly in the bill’s horizon, making an increase less likely. For 2026-01-24, the easing narrative still points lower, but uncertainty is higher at that longer horizon, so I move the probability back somewhat toward 0.5. I also assume the unknown 2025-10-26 comparison value is close to the 2025-10-24 reading, so baseline uncertainty only modestly affects the forecast.

Primary anchor: the DTB6 FRED tool gives a 0.64 probability of being above 3.65 by 2025-11-02, based on recent data that were very flat around 3.65-3.72. But I discount that materially because it is a pure time-series model and does not account for the major scheduled event immediately ahead: the Oct. 28-29 FOMC meeting. Before the cutoff, Reuters snippets indicated markets/economists overwhelmingly expected an October rate cut, with additional easing still expected later in 2025; another Reuters poll said short-dated Treasury yields were expected to edge lower on Fed-cut expectations. Since 6-month bill yields already embed expected policy, I do not go strongly below 0.5, but the event mix still makes an increase less likely than not.

I also adjust for the fact that the comparison value on 2025-10-26 is unknown and could end up a bit above the 10/24 reading of 3.65, which weakens the raw 0.64 anchor further. So I end up slightly NO-leaning on all three dates. Per the instruction that uncertainty should increase with horizon, I move the later probabilities closer to 0.5: near-term easing risk is clearest by 11/02, while by 2026-01-24 the path is much less certain, even though pre-cutoff consensus still leaned toward lower short rates rather than higher ones.

The series has clearly been trending down over the past year (about €6.40T in late 2024 to about €6.20T by late October 2025), and ECB/DNB materials before the cutoff say APP/PEPP reinvestments had ended and the Eurosystem balance sheet should keep shrinking, with portfolio run-off around €40B/month. That makes an increase by late November less likely than even odds. For late January, ongoing runoff and the December TLTRO maturity are still bearish, but year-end balance-sheet volatility/seasonality is material and the longer horizon adds uncertainty, so I pull the probability slightly back toward 0.5 rather than pushing it lower still. Final probabilities: 0.46, 0.40, 0.42.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool: for GASDESW, using data through 2025-10-20, it gives a combined estimate of 0.54 that the 2025-11-02 value exceeds 3.62, with recent momentum down but a still-positive medium-term trend. For later dates I adjust using the one allowed web search: EIA/press snippets before the cutoff indicate expectations for softer diesel prices into late 2025 and 2026 (including 2026 on-highway diesel below 2025 levels, helped by lower crude prices), which pushes against a sustained increase versus late October. Primary anchor is the FRED tool output for IC4WSA as of 2025-10-26: last known value before cutoff was 226,750 (2025-10-25 in the series), and the tool’s combined estimate for 2025-11-02 being above that threshold was 0.65. Primary anchor: FRED OVXCLS data through 2025-10-24 shows the latest known level at 38.7, which is the best proxy for the 2025-10-26 weekend reference value. The tool’s trend model gives P(OVX > 38.7 on 2025-11-02) = 0.63, supported by a modest recent uptrend. I trim that slightly to 0.59 because OVX is a volatility index and tends to mean-revert, and 38.7 is already somewhat elevated versus much of the past year. My one web search found late-October reporting emphasizing continued crude uncertainty from OPEC+, China demand, and geopolitics, which supports elevated near-term volatility, but also evidence of supply-glut / demand-softness forces that could cap or reduce volatility over the following month. So by 2025-11-25 I put the chance below even. By 2026-01-24, horizon uncertainty is larger, so I move back toward 0.5, while keeping a slight under-50 tilt because the starting level is relatively high and volatility indices often drift back down absent a fresh shock.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the key January 2025 AI Diffusion rule clearly would have qualified, but BIS announced on 2025-05-13 that it would rescind and not enforce that rule, while promising a future replacement. I found no evidence by the cutoff of a later Federal Register rule, EAR update, or Commerce/BIS announcement that actually implemented broad new or expanded controls on advanced AI chips, cloud access to AI compute, or AI model weights after May 1. The May 13 actions appear to be rescission plus guidance/policy statements, not qualifying broad new controls. So the question hinges on whether BIS is likely to implement a replacement in the remaining ~2 months of 2025. That is plausible because BIS explicitly said a replacement rule was coming and export-control rules can be issued quickly, including as interim final rules. But the lack of concrete action for more than five months after the rescission, plus the political and industry pushback that caused the rescission, materially lowers the odds. Net: somewhat below the 0.30 market estimate, but still meaningful due to the possibility of a late-year interim rule.

Low, but not near-zero. By 2025-10-26, almost all standalone AI-regulation bills with clearly qualifying language (e.g. chatbot/deepfake/liability/preemption proposals) were newly introduced and still in committee, leaving very little time for passage through both chambers and signature. The strongest broad-preemption effort already failed when the Senate stripped the AI moratorium 99-1, and reporting suggested Congress was unlikely to focus on AI-centric legislation in the remaining 2025 calendar. The main plausible path is a must-pass vehicle, especially the FY2026 NDAA, because House/Senate defense bills contained AI-specific procurement/security/risk-framework provisions that might be judged substantive regulation/authorization of regulation. But it is uncertain those provisions survive conference, are enacted before Jan. 1, and meet the market’s resolution standard. Net: a small but real chance, driven mostly by NDAA-type language, around 8%.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, there is no major reporting that Coalition of the Willing forces have already entered Ukraine. The strongest evidence points the other way: Reuters and related official statements describe the proposed reassurance/multinational force as a post-ceasefire or postwar arrangement, with plans ‘in place’ to deploy once hostilities have ceased, not before (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). By late October, Putin was still rejecting a ceasefire and Russia was escalating attacks, making a qualifying deployment in the remaining two months of 2025 unlikely. Even if a political breakthrough occurred, organizing an actual coalition-command presence on Ukrainian soil, in airspace, or territorial waters before year-end would be logistically and politically difficult. I therefore put this near the floor at 5%.

By 2025-10-26, the strongest evidence is the new NATO/PURL mechanism under which several European countries (including Germany, the Netherlands, Denmark, Norway, and Sweden) had already formally committed funding for U.S.-sourced weapons for Ukraine. Reuters on 2025-09-17 reported Zelensky saying the first batch would include Patriot missiles and HIMARS-related equipment; some reporting/snippets suggest HIMARS launchers themselves, while other summaries frame it as HIMARS missiles/ammunition only. Because the resolution requires launcher systems or functional substitutes, not just rockets, that ambiguity matters. Still, the base rate over the final two months is favorable: multiple European governments were actively financing U.S. weapons for Ukraine, rocket artillery remained a recognized gap, and substitute/compatible systems (M270/PULS/Chunmoo) were part of Europe’s wider procurement discussion. So I think the most likely outcome is YES, driven either by PURL being interpreted as launcher provision or by a further explicit late-2025 commitment—but not with certainty because public reporting before cutoff did not unambiguously confirm a launcher transfer.

Russian volumes look far below the 15 bcm/quarter threshold. The key constraint is pipeline capacity: after Ukraine transit ended, only TurkStream remains, and reporting indicates about 13.8 bcm reached Europe via TurkStream in Q1-Q3 2025 total, roughly 4.6 bcm per quarter, with capacity largely maxed out. That means any 15 bcm quarter would require about 10+ bcm of Russian LNG in that same quarter. But reported EU/Europe Russian LNG flows were only about 11 bcm in H1 2025 and roughly 14 bcm in Jan-Aug 2025, implying a quarterly LNG average around 5-6 bcm, nowhere near the level needed. Q1 2025 estimates were around 11-12 bcm total, and nothing suggests Q2 or Q3 came close. A winter Q4 spike is possible, but reaching 15 bcm would require an unusually large surge inconsistent with observed 2025 flow rates. So YES is very unlikely; I assign the minimum allowed 5%.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Musk looks like a strong favorite to still be #1 on Bloomberg’s index at year-end. Reuters reported that he was still the world’s richest person in mid-October and had become the first person above $500B in net worth on Oct. 1, with roughly a $150B cushion over #2 Larry Ellison at that time. The main risk is that the ranking is mark-to-market and Tesla is volatile: Ellison briefly passed Musk in September after Oracle’s huge AI-driven jump, showing that a flip is possible. But by October the lead had widened substantially again, and other rivals (e.g. Zuckerberg/Bezos) appeared much further back. With only about two months to resolution, Musk’s large lead plus the unlikelihood of another Oracle-sized shock makes YES clearly more likely than not, though not certain because of stock-market volatility and Bloomberg’s daily methodology.

As of 2025-10-26, I estimate a low but nontrivial chance of YES. The strongest argument against closure is revealed preference: during the much more acute June 2025 crisis—after Israeli and then U.S. strikes on Iran, a parliamentary vote favoring closure, shipping diversions, and elevated maritime alerts—Iran still did not actually halt or severely restrict transit. Analysts repeatedly judged a full closure unlikely because it would damage Iran’s own exports, alienate China and Gulf neighbors, and likely trigger major military retaliation. Historical precedent also favors threats over execution. The main argument for YES is that by late September/October 2025 UN snapback sanctions were reimposed and Iranian officials renewed explicit Hormuz closure threats tied to inspections or efforts to choke off Iranian oil exports. Given the market’s broad resolution criteria, a targeted interdiction of Western shipping or a short, intense disruption causing significant difficulty for oil flows could suffice even without a total shutdown. Still, with only about two months remaining after the cutoff, and no evidence by then of an actual sustained operational move to close the strait, 10% best fits the balance of incentives, precedent, and short horizon.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, India and Pakistan had already gone through their sharpest crisis of the year in May—four days of missile, drone, air, and artillery clashes—yet neither side formally declared war. Instead, they accepted a ceasefire, military-to-military talks, and subsequent troop reduction/de-escalation (search_0_summary, search_1_summary). That is strong revealed evidence: even under acute pressure, both governments avoided the exact act this market requires. The criterion is also unusually strict: border clashes, strikes, and proxy conflict do not count; only an official public declaration does. Historical/context reporting in 2025 also notes that formal declarations have often not been used in India-Pakistan conflicts, making this pathway especially unlikely. With only about two months remaining after the cutoff, the base rate for a formal declaration is very low. I would ordinarily put this a bit below 5%, but given the required probability bounds, I submit 5%.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Musk is still actively serving as Tesla CEO and was leading the Q3 earnings call on Oct. 22 while discussing near-term Tesla plans for the rest of 2025 (search_2_summary). The remaining horizon is only about two months. The strongest evidence points to continuity: Tesla’s chair publicly denied a CEO search and said the board is highly confident in Musk; Musk said in May he expected to remain CEO for at least five more years; and Tesla’s 2025 compensation/succession framework is built around Musk continuing as CEO or a top executive (search_1_summary, search_3_summary). Counterevidence exists — the WSJ-reported succession search, claims he had said he no longer wanted the role, shareholder pressure for a succession plan, and governance/legal concerns — but these look more like medium-term governance friction than signs of an imminent departure before year-end (search_0_summary, search_3_summary). Given the short time left and Musk’s entrenched control, YES seems very unlikely, though not impossible.

As of 2025-10-26, Tesla had only a small Austin robotaxi operation, with credible reporting still describing human safety monitors in at least many rides, while California driverless expansion was not close and other states were still in early permitting stages. The main path to YES is Austin: if Tesla removes in-car monitors soon enough and keeps the service running through Dec. 31, then 10,000 bona fide rides is operationally achievable with even a modest fleet. But the strict market criteria make this harder than headline 'robotaxi launch' claims: no observers in the car, no required human backup/teleoperation, real rider trips, and active on the last day of the year. Musk's late-October claim that monitors would be removed by year-end is evidence in favor, but it also implies this milestone was not yet clearly achieved by the cutoff. Given the remaining ~2 months, regulatory/safety risk, and ambiguity over whether any current rides already qualify, I estimate about a one-in-three chance.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly favors YES. Statistics Korea’s preliminary 2024 annual total fertility rate already rose to 0.75 from 0.72 in 2023. Then 2025 data stayed above the threshold: Q1 2025 TFR was 0.82 vs 0.77 a year earlier, Q2 was about 0.75, births were up 6.9% in Jan-May and 7.4% in H1, and July marked a 13th consecutive monthly increase in births (search_1_summary, search_4_summary). Marriages had also surged 14.9% in 2024, which is a strong leading indicator for births. Officials were publicly projecting roughly 0.79-0.80 for 2025. Since the bar is only 0.72, the annual 2025 rate would need an implausibly sharp collapse in late 2025 to finish below it. Residual uncertainty remains from possible revisions, denominator effects, or an unexpected late-year reversal, but these seem small.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Musk is still Tesla CEO, the board has publicly backed him, denied a replacement search, and late-2025 proxy/pay materials are structured around retaining him rather than replacing him. Musk also said in May he intended to remain CEO for years, and October proxy-related filings still list him as an active executive/director ahead of the November annual meeting. With only about two months left in 2025, a departure would likely require a sudden shock: board revolt after further business/governance deterioration, a legal/regulatory event, health/personal issues, or an abrupt voluntary exit. Those paths exist but look unlikely given the board’s demonstrated alignment with Musk and the short horizon. I’d put YES near the floor at 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, the bar for YES is quite high: not just strikes, but a publicly acknowledged, sustained US-Iran war. The strongest evidence is that even the June 2025 direct US strikes on Iranian nuclear sites and Iran’s retaliation still quickly moved to a ceasefire and were not treated as an ongoing US-Iran war. By October, tensions remained elevated—snapback sanctions, stalled nuclear talks, proxy activity, and Iranian threats—but there was no clear sustained campaign, no major ground deployment, and US force posture in Iraq was being reduced rather than surged. With only about two months left in 2025, the main path to YES is a fresh major escalation (for example a deadly proxy attack on Americans or a Hormuz/naval crisis) that then persists long enough to create media and official consensus of ‘war’. That tail risk is real but small, so I put it at 6%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, San Diego FC had already officially clinched an MLS Cup Playoffs berth and was listed by MLS as the No. 1 seed in the Western Conference / part of the 18-team playoff field (search_1_summary). This is no longer a forward-looking performance question by the cutoff date; it is effectively already determined unless some highly unusual retroactive sanction or resolution-criteria quirk overturns their qualification. So the probability is at the practical ceiling.

I estimate a 5% chance. By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the only credible near-term path to a human lunar landing was NASA’s Artemis III, and NASA’s own updated schedule had already slipped that mission to mid-2027, with Artemis II (a required crewed lunar flyby test) delayed to April 2026 (search_0_summary). China’s crewed lunar program remained officially targeted for before 2030, with major hardware still in development and no indication of a 2025 attempt (search_1_summary). Private SpaceX/dearMoon plans were canceled and in any case were circumlunar, not a landing. So a landing by end-2025 would require a dramatic, unsignaled acceleration or an entirely unexpected new entrant, which looks extremely unlikely. I would go lower absent the platform floor, but within the allowed range I submit 5%.

As of the cutoff, this looks very unlikely. The strongest evidence is simply the price path: TSLA was about $433.72 on Oct. 24, 2025, and even its 52-week low as of Aug. 20 was $202.59 (search_1_summary, search_4_summary). So by late October, the stock had never actually broken $200 in 2025, and from the latest level it would need to fall more than 54% in roughly two months to do so. Tesla’s fundamentals were clearly mixed-to-weak—declining earnings/margins, market-share losses in key regions, and some bearish analyst commentary (search_1_summary). Technicals also showed some short-term weakness after Q3 earnings, and volatility was elevated, but the options-implied move around earnings was only about +/-7.9% (search_4_summary), nowhere near what would be needed. A sub-$200 print before year-end would likely require a severe company-specific shock or broader market crash. That kind of tail risk is not zero, but it is small. Because probabilities must stay above 0.05 here, I submit 5%.

Trump had already won by the cutoff, so this is effectively asking whether the BLS headline CPI inflation rate reported around Dec. 10, 2025 will be below 2.5%. As of the latest available BLS release before cutoff, September 2025 CPI was 3.0% y/y, with 0.3% m/m after 0.4% in August. To get below 2.5% by the late-2025 release would likely require unusually flat or negative inflation over the remaining months, because late-2024 base effects were not especially favorable (Nov. 2024 CPI was already 2.7% y/y with a 0.3% monthly rise). Most contemporaneous analyst commentary pointed the other way: JPMorgan expected CPI to rise toward 3.5% in 4Q 2025, corporate economists had revised 2025 CPI forecasts up to 2.7%, and St. Louis Fed/Reuters pieces noted tariff effects already adding roughly 0.3pp with risk of more ahead. There is some chance from cooling shelter or energy weakness, but sub-2.5% by resolution looks unlikely.

Trump already did not win the 2025 Nobel Peace Prize, so this market resolves YES iff there is no bilateral Ukraine ceasefire before 2026. As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, there was still active fighting; Reuters reported on Oct. 21-22 that Russia rejected an immediate ceasefire, reiterated harder territorial demands, and a planned Trump-Putin summit was put on hold. Earlier 2025 diplomacy produced only limited/partial truces and repeated failed 30-day ceasefire pushes, not a broad bilateral ceasefire. With only about two months left in the year, some chance remains for a short holiday or politically driven temporary truce, but the base rate from the current diplomatic/military position looks low—around 8%. Therefore YES (no ceasefire, matching Nobel NO) is about 92%.

I found no evidence that a Waymo-related SNL sketch had already aired by the 2025-10-26 cutoff, despite searching for Waymo/SNL combinations and episode recaps. That pushes most of the probability mass onto the remaining late-2025 episodes only. Waymo was culturally salient in 2025 because of robotaxi growth, viral incidents, and the broader Tesla-vs.-Waymo robotaxi story, so it is plausible SNL could use it in a tech or transportation sketch. But SNL has relatively few episodes left in the calendar year, and most sketches draw from a huge pool of political and pop-culture material; Waymo is still a narrower topic than Tesla/Elon. Starting from the market’s 0.17 and updating slightly downward because there is still no sign it had happened by late October, I estimate a 14% chance of a Waymo skit appearing sometime in the remainder of 2025.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Musk had clearly announced the idea of an 'America Party' in early July, and media often described this as a launch. But the stronger evidence points the other way for resolution under this market’s clarified standards: he disavowed purported FEC filings as fake, there was no verified formal filing tied to him, and multiple later reports said he had taken no visible concrete steps such as building party infrastructure, a website/platform, or other formal organization. Late-summer reporting also indicated he was putting the brakes on the plan and refocusing on his companies. Since the creator said a tweet/announcement alone is not enough, and tangible evidence is required, the party did not appear established by the cutoff. There was still some chance he could create minimal infrastructure before Dec. 31, 2025, because the bar is lower than ballot access, but with only about two months left and no momentum, that chance looks small. Hence about 8%.

By 2025-10-26, Iran faced serious stressors—postwar security disruption, accelerated succession planning around Khamenei, renewed sanctions, recession/inflation risk, and broad public dissatisfaction. But the evidence still points to regime survival through year-end: Reuters reported no significant anti-regime protests even after the June war; the state responded with mass arrests, executions, and deployments; expert assessments said coercive capacity would likely prevent collapse; and the IRGC/Basij structure is specifically designed to deter coups and suppress unrest. Also, ordinary succession within the system would resolve NO, not YES. With only about two months left in 2025, true loss of control in Tehran looked possible but very unlikely.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly suggests hallucinations had improved but were not close to being generally regarded as 'fixed.' Benchmarks cited in 2025 showed low rates on some narrow tasks (sometimes ~1-3%, occasionally lower), but much higher rates on grounded, medical, and adversarial settings (often ~6-16%, and far worse in some specialized studies). Multiple 2025 sources—including OpenAI-linked research and surveys—still explicitly described hallucinations as persistent, structurally tied to current training/evaluation incentives, and only partially mitigated by techniques like RAG, CoT, and refusal behavior. Since the resolution is subjective ('has the problem been fixed?'), the bar for YES is high: not just improvement, but broad disappearance of the outstanding problem. With only about two months remaining after the cutoff and the public/research consensus still saying the problem exists, YES looks very unlikely, though not impossible if a sudden breakthrough or evaluator-friendly interpretation emerged.

As of 2025-10-26, the case appears active and contentious rather than near resolution. Independent docket summaries show: filed 2025-02-18; TRO/preliminary-injunction litigation in spring; ongoing evidentiary disputes; no clear notice of settlement or stay for settlement; and on 2025-09-04 the court allowed an amended complaint, with defendant still expected to pursue jurisdictional/dismissal challenges. That posture usually points to more litigation, not an imminent deal. There is also no clear evidence of a scheduled mediation or settlement conference before year-end. Against that, civil cases often do settle, and the privacy/reputational nature of the dispute gives some incentive to avoid trial. But with only about two months left after the cutoff, the lack of settlement signals makes YES fairly unlikely. I therefore put settlement by the end of 2025 at 13%.

By the best pre-cutoff evidence, Nigeria was nowhere near 95% account ownership by end-2024. EFInA’s 2023 survey put total financial inclusion at 74%, formal financial-service use at 64%, and specifically bank-account ownership at 52%. Later 2024 data cited from World Bank Global Findex 2025 put Nigeria’s account ownership at 63.3% in 2024. Multiple contemporaneous reports explicitly said Nigeria remained far from the CBN’s 95% target. A 30+ point miss is too large to be explained by definitional or sampling differences, and if no qualifying CBN data appears by the deadline the question also resolves NO. So YES looks extremely unlikely; I use the minimum allowed probability of 5% rather than something closer to zero.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, there is substantial political opposition to solar geoengineering, but almost all of it is still at the level of COP decisions, ministerial declarations, advocacy for a future non-use agreement, or support for negotiations—not an actual treaty obligation by 10+ countries. The strongest evidence is: (1) the CBD has a long-standing ‘de facto moratorium’ on geoengineering, reaffirmed in 2024, but multiple sources describe it as a precautionary COP decision rather than a clear new binding treaty ban that would obviously satisfy this market; (2) AMCEN in July 2025 and many Global South countries called for a non-use agreement, but sources explicitly frame this as urging negotiation, not concluding one; (3) no search result identified a signed or ratified diplomatic agreement specifically restricting solar geoengineering by states. With only about two months left until 2026, the base rate for negotiating, signing, and creating a qualifying treaty obligation across at least ten countries is extremely low. I assign a floor-level but nonzero probability in case an existing multilateral instrument is judged to count unexpectedly, or a fast multilateral commitment is reached late in 2025.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence falls far short of the question’s very strict criteria. There is a peer-reviewed 2024 Chemical Geology paper on IM1 spherules, but available summaries indicate it argues unusual composition / possible extrasolar origin, not a 95%-confidence technological origin. A 2025 peer-reviewed re-evaluation reportedly argues many or most spherules are volcanic/terrestrial, which cuts strongly against consensus. I found no evidence by the cutoff of a qualifying systematic review that (1) uses at least 3 independent labs, (2) concludes with 95% confidence the material is non-Earth, and (3) concludes with 95% confidence that no natural process can explain it. With only about two months remaining until the publication deadline and strong active dispute, a YES resolution looks extraordinarily unlikely. I assign the minimum allowed probability, 5%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence suggests Tesla had already crossed the 50%-below-ATH threshold in March 2025, but I found no sign that three other Magnificent Seven names had done so. The closest others cited were much milder drawdowns: Nvidia and Meta were around 20% off highs in March, and broad market conditions improved afterward, with Wall Street indexes posting record highs again in September 2025. Through Q3 2025 the Mag 7 were still driving a large share of S&P 500 gains, which is inconsistent with a multi-name 50% collapse already being underway. For YES to resolve from this point, three additional stocks among Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, and Meta would need to crash to at least 50% below their historical highs within only about two months. That is possible in an extreme recession/liquidity shock or sudden AI/earnings unwind, but it would require a very severe and rapid selloff across multiple mega-cap leaders. So the probability is very low; constrained by the instruction floor, I submit 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, the strongest US-specific evidence still centers on CDC-linked figures around 35,000 annual deaths from antibiotic-resistant infections, not 70,000. Searches found no credible US report already at or above the threshold, and late-2024/2025 coverage about worsening resistance discussed surges in specific pathogens rather than a revised national death total anywhere near double. Global Lancet/GRAM work shows AMR is a growing threat, but it does not provide a US estimate that clearly resolves this market, and there is little time left before 2025 ends for CDC or another credible source to publish a dramatically higher US annual figure. A surprise upward methodological revision is possible, but it looks unlikely.

Para el 26 de octubre de 2025, la evidencia oficial apunta casi decisivamente a NO. Rockstar anunció el 2 de mayo de 2025 que GTA VI pasó de otoño de 2025 a una fecha concreta de lanzamiento: 26 de mayo de 2026. Además, Take-Two siguió confirmando esa fecha en comunicaciones posteriores de 2025. Dado que Rockstar normalmente hace lanzamientos coordinados y no hay señales creíbles de una venta anticipada solo en Europa, la probabilidad de que estuviera a la venta en Europa en 2025 es extremadamente baja. Dejo un 5% residual por la pequeña posibilidad de un cambio extraordinario, reversión del retraso o una interpretación operativa inusual de disponibilidad, pero el resultado más probable es claramente NO.

A 26 de octubre de 2025, la evidencia favorece un cierre anual de BTC por encima de $93.429,20. Reuters reportó un máximo histórico por encima de $125k a inicios de octubre, una corrección fuerte hasta ~$104,8k tras liquidaciones récord y tensiones macro, y luego una recuperación hacia ~$111k el 25 de octubre. Eso deja a Bitcoin ~15-16% por encima del umbral con solo algo más de dos meses por delante. Ese margen no es enorme para un activo tan volátil, así que un NO sigue siendo muy plausible: una caída de 15-20% en semanas es totalmente normal en BTC, y además hubo fuerte compra de puts/hedges tras la sacudida de octubre. Pero el contexto de fondo seguía siendo alcista: demanda institucional fuerte, ETF inflows récord a comienzos de octubre, nuevos productos cripto en camino y un entorno regulatorio estadounidense más favorable. En conjunto, veo más probable que Bitcoin mantenga suficiente colchón para terminar el año por encima del nivel inicial, aunque no con certeza extrema.

Erin’s 160 mph peak sets a very high bar: a later Atlantic hurricane would likely need to reach at least 165 mph (145 kt in NHC-style increments). As of Oct. 26, the only realistic candidate is Hurricane Melissa. NHC has Melissa at 145 mph and forecasts a peak of 160 mph, but notes that forecast is already on the high side of guidance, and Melissa is nearing Jamaica/Cuba with land interaction and later increasing shear expected to limit strengthening. So Melissa getting to 165+ mph is plausible, but clearly less likely than not. Beyond Melissa, NHC says no new tropical cyclone formation is expected over the next 7 days, leaving only a short, climatologically unfavorable late-season window for any new storm to form and become an extreme Category 5. Net: mostly a ‘no,’ but Melissa creates a meaningful upside tail above the earlier market level.

Chegg looked badly disrupted by AI as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff: revenue was down ~36% YoY in Q2 2025, subscribers were falling sharply, Q3 guidance was weak, and management was restructuring and exploring strategic alternatives. Those are real distress signals. But a bankruptcy filing before 2026 still seemed unlikely because the near-term balance-sheet picture was not yet consistent with an imminent filing: Chegg had about $114M in cash/investments at end-Q2 2025, only about $62M of remaining convert debt, and those 2026 notes mature on Sept. 1, 2026, i.e. after the resolution date. It had already repaid the 2025 notes and repurchased part of the 2026 notes at a discount. With positive remaining liquidity, no obvious pre-2026 maturity wall, and only about two months left until resolution, the base case is continued shrinkage / sale / turnaround efforts rather than a bankruptcy petition. I therefore put the chance at a low but nontrivial 8%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the strongest relevant LFU tensions still appear to be well below discovery threshold: the famous LHCb RK/RK* anomaly had already largely faded with newer data, while charged-current anomalies such as R(D) and R(D*) were still being described in 2025 reviews and summaries as roughly ~3.1–3.3σ globally, not 5σ. Belle II and other 2024–2025 peer-reviewed measurements were reported as broadly consistent with the Standard Model or with existing world averages, not as discovery-level violations. I found no evidence of any peer-reviewed 2025 paper already claiming >=5σ LFU violation, and with only about two months remaining until the 2025-12-31 publication deadline, both obtaining such a result and getting it peer-reviewed in time looked very unlikely. Because the instructions forbid probabilities below 0.05, I submit the floor value.

Late-October evidence makes Sherrill the favorite but not a lock. Most recent polls had her ahead by about 5-7 points and near 50% (Rutgers/Eagleton 50-45, Quinnipiac 50-44, FDU 52-45), though Emerson showed a tie, suggesting a real but not overwhelming lead. Structural factors also help her: New Jersey still has a large Democratic registration advantage, analysts rated the race Leans Democratic, and a Republican White House likely blunts the usual anti-incumbent-party dynamic against Democrats in NJ. Offsetting that, Ciattarelli had stronger enthusiasm, off-year turnout is less predictable, and New Jersey polling badly understated him in 2021, so a polling-error upset is plausible. Net: Sherrill is clearly favored, but the chance of a Ciattarelli win remains substantial.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, FY 2025 had already ended and the strongest available pre-cutoff sources already gave near-final numbers. The final September 2025 Monthly Treasury Statement and multiple contemporaneous summaries (including CBO/AAF writeups) indicate FY 2025 customs duties were about $195 billion, up roughly $118 billion from FY 2024 but still far below the $500 billion lower bound for this bracket (search_0_summary, search_2 results). Even allowing for later Treasury Financial Report revisions or accounting differences, moving from about $195B to above $500B would require an enormous upward revision that is not plausible. The publication-timing clause also does not help YES, because Treasury normally publishes the Financial Report by mid-January (FY 2024 report was published 2025-01-16 per search #3), and if it were not published by 2026-02-28 the market resolves to the lowest bracket anyway. So YES looked essentially impossible as of the cutoff; I assign the floor probability of 5%.

Anthropic looked genuinely competitive as of the cutoff: multiple pre-10/26 sources reported Claude Sonnet 4.5 at or effectively tied for the top of the LMArena text leaderboard in early October, with Gemini 2.5 Pro extremely close and Claude Opus 4.1 also near the top (search_2_summary). That means Anthropic was not a long shot from the current state, and the market’s alphabetical tiebreak helps Anthropic in any exact tie. 

But the top spot had been very volatile in 2025: Gemini 2.5 Pro surged to #1 in March, GPT-5 was reported sweeping categories in August, and then Claude Sonnet 4.5 moved to the top cluster by October. With ~2 months still to go, it was quite plausible that Google (rumored Gemini 3) or OpenAI would launch/update another frontier model before year-end, and either could overtake a very narrow Anthropic lead (search_2_summary, search_4_summary). 

So Anthropic had a real path via (a) holding its current lead/tie, or (b) releasing a stronger Claude update, but it was still less likely than the field because of expected late-year competition and the leaderboard’s rapid turnover. Net: about 24%.

Johannes Kaiser looked very unlikely to finish first as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff. Late-October reporting and poll summaries consistently had him well behind the top two: around 11-13% for Kaiser versus roughly 20-27% for José Antonio Kast and 26-33% for Jeannette Jara, with Evelyn Matthei also often ahead or near Kaiser. An October 21 poll average cited Kaiser at 11.95%, compared with Jara 29.54% and Kast 23.26%; a mid-October LatamPulse poll had Kaiser at 13.4% versus Jara 32.7% and Kast 20.1%. No credible late poll found Kaiser leading, and with only about three weeks left he would need an extraordinary polling miss or sudden realignment to overtake both Jara and Kast. I leave a small tail for late-campaign volatility, underestimation of outsider/right-populist support, and fragmentation on the right, but overall his chance to win the first round appears very low.

Brooklyn looked like one of the NBA’s weakest teams as of 2025-10-26. Preseason projections had them at just 19.5-20.5 wins and only about a 19.7% chance to make the postseason, with multiple previews placing them in the clear bottom tier of the league and likely worst in the East (search_0_summary). Futures markets were even harsher: FanDuel listed the Nets at +50000 to win the Eastern Conference, the longest odds in the league and behind every other East team, while Cleveland, New York, and Orlando were the main conference favorites (search_1_summary, search_2_summary). Even with injuries weakening some usual East powers like Boston, Indiana, and Philadelphia, analysts still did not treat Brooklyn as a plausible beneficiary; other teams were named instead (search_2_summary). So the true chance is extremely small; I’m submitting the minimum allowed 5%.

Moonshot looks unlikely to be #1 on Oct. 31. Evidence available before the Oct. 26 cutoff suggests Moonshot’s Kimi K2 was strong and in/near the top five, but not currently leading; a late-October report had Z.ai’s GLM-4.6 at #1 with DeepSeek and Qwen also ahead, while Moonshot was only described as top-five. With only ~5 days from cutoff to resolution, Moonshot would probably need either a major new model release plus very rapid Arena uptake, or a sizable leaderboard reshuffle. That can happen, but it is uncommon, and several strong competitors are already ahead. I therefore assign a low but non-negligible chance.

By 2025-10-26, a YES outcome looks extremely unlikely. Section 4 requires JD Vance plus a majority of Trump’s Cabinet to declare him unable to serve, and if Trump contests it, two-thirds of both chambers of Congress must sustain that determination. The evidence available before the cutoff shows public calls from opponents, but no credible signs that Vance or a Cabinet largely composed of Trump loyalists is preparing to act; coverage describes them as aligned with him, and Congress is not situated for a two-thirds anti-Trump vote. A recent White House/Reuters-reported medical evaluation also said Trump was in 'exceptional health,' reducing the chance of a sudden incapacity narrative. With only about two months left in 2025 and Section 4 never having been successfully used before, the path to resolution is possible but very remote.

Russia has increased military infrastructure near Finland and continues hostile rhetoric and hybrid pressure, but the evidence available by 2025-10-26 points to a medium/long-term threat, not an imminent 2025 invasion. Finnish and Western reporting indicates Russia is rebuilding forces near the border, yet Finnish officials and President Stubb downplayed any immediate threat, and ISW explicitly said it had not observed indicators of active preparation for an imminent conflict with NATO. Russia also remained heavily committed in Ukraine, with poor 2025 offensive results, heavy losses, and equipment constraints. An overt move into Finland would mean direct war with NATO, a huge escalation for little obvious short-term gain. Grey-zone sabotage and migration pressure are real, but the market requires an offensive intended to establish control over territory, and there was no persuasive sign of such an operation before year-end. Given the very short remaining horizon from the cutoff to Dec. 31, YES looks very unlikely, though not impossible.

As of 2025-10-26, headline CPI had reached only 3.0% y/y in September after 2.9% in August (search_0_summary, search_3_summary). The only months left that can trigger resolution are October, November, and December 2025. Late-2024 base effects are not favorable enough to make 4.1% easy: Oct/Nov/Dec 2024 headline CPI had monthly increases of about 0.2%, 0.3%, and 0.4% respectively (search_1_summary). Starting from 3.0% y/y in September, getting to 4.1% would require an unusually large acceleration over the final 1-3 reports—roughly around 1.3% in October alone, or about 2.0% cumulative across Oct-Dec, which is far above recent 2025 inflation momentum. Tariffs are creating some upward pressure and forecasters noted inflation was sticky, but available October commentary still described inflation as not ‘running wild,’ and the September print was lower than expected (search_3_summary). So YES is possible only under a sharp late-year inflation surprise, but that looks unlikely. I put it slightly below the 10/16 market price and above the floor because tariff pass-through and BLS data-quality/shutdown issues leave some tail risk.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the Chiefs look like a strong but not dominant Super Bowl contender. Relevant evidence: (1) preseason/early-season outright odds had Kansas City around +800 to +850, implying roughly a 10-12% title chance before vig adjustments (search_4_summary); (2) by Oct. 21 they were only 4-3, which hurts their path to a top AFC seed and likely means more playoff road games (search_3_summary); (3) despite the mediocre record, they had just rebounded with convincing wins over Detroit and Las Vegas, Rashee Rice had returned, Mahomes/Reid remain intact, and AP still described them as championship contenders (search_2_summary, search_3_summary); (4) playoff odds were reportedly still very high (~93%), so elimination risk was meaningful but not large. Balancing their elite quarterback/coaching pedigree and high playoff likelihood against a weaker regular-season position and strong rivals (Bills, Ravens, Eagles), I put their Super Bowl win probability slightly above the cited 0.12 market estimate: 13%.

Matt Salsberg was confirmed in the 64-player field, so his ex ante chance was only about 1.6% before skill adjustments. He is a credible player, but by the 2025-10-26 cutoff the strongest available evidence points away from him: multiple pre-cutoff reports said the already-played August 1–3 event had effectively been leaked, with Sam Soverel identified as the presumed winner and trading around 90%+ / even 99% on outside markets (search_1_summary, search #2 snippets). I found no contrary reporting suggesting Salsberg made a deep run. Since the tournament was already completed and credible reporting heavily favored another named player, Salsberg’s true chance was very close to zero; I submit 5% as the minimum allowed under the rules.

As of 2025-10-26, Avatar: Fire and Ash looked like a real Best Picture nomination contender but not a leading winner. Multiple awards-prediction outlets had it in or around the top 10, helped by James Cameron’s track record and the fact that both prior Avatar films made Best Picture. But it was still unreleased/under embargo, and most pundit coverage emphasized it more as a major craft/Visual Effects player than as a top-flight Best Picture frontrunner. In October 2025 the apparent leaders were other films such as One Battle After Another, Sinners, Hamnet, Frankenstein, and others. So the movie had a decent chance to be nominated, but only a small chance to actually win if nominated. Combining a moderate nomination probability with a low win-given-nomination probability yields roughly 7% overall.

Eminem looked very unlikely to finish 2025 as Spotify’s most-streamed artist based on pre-10/26 evidence. The strongest indicators all pointed elsewhere: betting coverage called Taylor Swift the clear favorite; Eminem was reported only around 7th in 2025 overall streams by mid-April and behind Drake among rappers; and monthly-listener snapshots put him well below leaders like Taylor Swift, The Weeknd, and Bruno Mars. I found no solid evidence of a major late-2025 Eminem release big enough to overturn that gap with only about two months left in the year. Market sentiment near zero is directionally consistent. I’d put this at the floor-like low end: 5%.

As of 2025-10-26, NATO had not invoked Article 5 despite the strongest apparent test case of the year: repeated Russian drone/airspace violations affecting Poland and other Allies in September-October. Official NATO action was Article 4 consultation, not Article 5, and NATO statements emphasized flexible military/non-military responses rather than collective-defence declaration. Reuters/expert coverage indicates the threshold for Article 5 remains very high, especially for ambiguous or hybrid incidents; a more likely trigger would be a clearly attributable, deliberate, and probably fatal attack on NATO territory. With only about two months left in the resolution window, and given Russia’s apparent interest in probing below the threshold of direct NATO war plus intelligence assessments that conventional attack risk remains low, YES is possible but still quite unlikely. I therefore estimate a 5% chance of an Article 5 invocation before 2025-12-31.

Billie Eilish looked very unlikely to finish as Spotify’s #1 artist for 2025 as of 2025-10-26. The strongest signals pointed elsewhere: Spotify Wrapped 2024 had her only 5th globally behind Taylor Swift, The Weeknd, Bad Bunny, and Drake, and late-2025 betting/prediction coverage identified Bad Bunny as the favorite with Taylor Swift the main challenger, not Billie. Billie did have very strong monthly-listener numbers in 2025, which keeps a small upset path alive, but monthly listeners are a weak proxy for total yearly streams and she had no clear evidence of leading year-to-date volume. With most of the Wrapped tracking window already elapsed by late October, her chance looked only about one-in-twenty.

OpenAI had not made an official AGI-achievement announcement by the 2025-10-26 cutoff, leaving only about two months for a YES resolution. The strongest pro-YES evidence is Sam Altman’s rhetoric: in Jan. 2025 he said OpenAI is 'confident we know how to build AGI as we have traditionally understood it,' and he has repeatedly suggested rapid progress toward AGI/superintelligence. That makes a late-2025 declaration conceivable. But the stronger evidence points against an announcement by year-end: OpenAI’s own official materials in Feb./Mar. 2025 still describe AGI as a future goal and talk about people coming to OpenAI 'to build AGI,' not having already achieved it; no official announcement had appeared by late October despite multiple major product and publicity moments; and reporting indicates an AGI declaration would have major Microsoft-contract consequences, creating a substantial incentive to avoid a formal declaration unless unavoidable. Since the market resolves on an explicit announcement rather than technical reality, the key question is willingness to say it publicly.
